critic_raw:
Section 4 explicitly states that preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. For a ClinicalBERT fine-tuning task, the tokenizer vocabulary is pretrained and fixed, but any custom vocabulary expansions, truncation thresholds calibrated to the corpus, or domain-specific normalization statistics reflect the full 3-year patient corpus including test-period clinical notes. This encodes test-distribution language properties into the preprocessing pipeline.

Section 6 contains explicit test set contamination: 'a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' The stated purpose — ensuring against overfitting to the validation set and checking generalization — is precisely what the test set is supposed to provide through a single final evaluation. The described 'cross-verification' means the test set performance is observed before the final model is locked, and any implicit decision made based on this observation (even if 'do nothing' is the decision) constitutes test-set use during development. The phrase 'provide confidence that the configuration will generalize' is a rationalization for peeking.

The temporal split is used correctly (chronological ordering with 60/20/20 by patient count). However, with 100,000 patients split temporally, the training covers approximately 22 months, validation 7 months, and test 7 months. For rare complication classes, 7 months of test data may contain very few examples of rare complications. Macro-averaged F1 on the test set for a 5-class problem with rare classes and small absolute test counts will have wide confidence intervals that the design does not characterize.

ClinicalBERT is fine-tuned end-to-end while the baseline uses TF-IDF. Fine-tuned transformer representations are task-specific learned embeddings while TF-IDF is a fixed statistical representation. The comparison simultaneously tests (a) learned vs. fixed text representations and (b) contextual vs. bag-of-words encoding. Either of these alone could explain a performance difference. The design does not include a frozen ClinicalBERT + logistic regression baseline that would isolate the representation learning contribution from the classification architecture contribution.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-period clinical note language properties into normalization and vocabulary parameters used during training.

Section 6 explicitly describes a 'cross-verification on the test set' to confirm hyperparameter performance before finalizing the model — this is a test-set peek that invalidates the claimed unbiased final evaluation.

For rare post-discharge complications with only 7 months of test data, macro-averaged F1 will have very wide confidence intervals from small absolute class counts, and the design provides no minimum sample size analysis for reliable per-class estimation.

ClinicalBERT fine-tuned end-to-end is compared against TF-IDF logistic regression — this simultaneously tests learned vs. fixed representations and contextual vs. bag-of-words encoding, making any performance difference unattributable to a single factor.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-period clinical note language properties into normalization and vocabulary parameters used during training.

Section 6 explicitly describes a 'cross-verification on the test set' to confirm hyperparameter performance before finalizing the model — this is a test-set peek that invalidates the claimed unbiased final evaluation.

For rare post-discharge complications with only 7 months of test data, macro-averaged F1 will have very wide confidence intervals from small absolute class counts, and the design provides no minimum sample size analysis for reliable per-class estimation.

ClinicalBERT fine-tuned end-to-end is compared against TF-IDF logistic regression — this simultaneously tests learned vs. fixed representations and contextual vs. bag-of-words encoding, making any performance difference unattributable to a single factor.
